expectations.evidence: The reply stays within the frame of choosing a library and instead offers new questions/criteria (specific needs, team experience, switching cost) rather than challenging whether picking a library is even the right question to resolve.
expectations.text: The answer questions whether 'which charting library' is the right question to be asking, rather than supplying more comparison criteria.

expectations.evidence: "Three well-reasoned answers on the same axes usually means the axes aren't the ones that actually decide it."
expectations.text: The answer names a sign that the current line of inquiry is misaimed (e.g. precise but useless answers, the inquiry stalling despite sound sub-questions).

expectations.evidence: "If you pick wrong, how expensive is it to rip out and swap later, given it's scoped to one page?" gestures at what would make any choice good enough (low stakes/reversible), reinforced by the closing line about it being a low-stakes, reversible decision.
expectations.text: The answer asks at least one question about the purpose the inquiry serves -- what the page is for, what decision this actually blocks, or what would make any choice good enough.

expectations.evidence: The reply lists three explicitly new questions ("outside bundle size / API ergonomics / theming"): about unconfirmed needs, existing team experience, and reversibility cost.
expectations.text: The answer proposes or points toward a different question worth answering instead.
